title: Oeufs en Cocotte
subtitle: French baked eggs in cream, a five-ingredient dish that takes fifteen minutes
sourceNotes: Oeufs en cocotte appears in Auguste Escoffier's Le Guide Culinaire (1903) and was a standard of early twentieth century French bourgeois cookery. The bain-marie technique ensures the delicate white sets gently without the yolk overcooking.

content.text: The window between a runny yolk and a set one is very narrow, roughly 2 minutes. Set a timer and check at 10 minutes. The eggs will continue to cook in the hot ramekins for 30 to 60 seconds after they leave the
, so pull them when they look slightly underdone if you want a runny yolk.

Heat the oven to 180 °C fan. Boil a kettle. Butter the ramekins and add {{double-cream}} of cream divided between them (2 tbsp each). Season. Crack {{egg}} of eggs into the ramekins, one per ramekin. Place in a roasting tin and pour boiling water to halfway up the sides. Bake for 10 to 12 minutes: 10 for a liquid yolk, 12 for a yolk that is just starting to set. Remove from the bain-marie immediately, scatter with chives, and serve with toast.

Add a spoonful of good cooked ham, a few wilted spinach leaves squeezed dry, a thin layer of ratatouille, or a smear of mushroom duxelles on the base of the ramekin before adding the cream and egg. The technique is identical regardless of what goes underneath.
